Author: Michael Normyle
Summary: AI fears caused market selloff, but positive news from Anthropic boosted some software stocks. NVIDIA’s strong earnings highlighted ongoing AI chip demand, though its stock declined over concerns about demand sustainability. Investors sought safety amid geopolitical tensions, lowering Treasury yields and leaving the Nasdaq-100 flat. Upcoming events include manufacturing data, earnings reports, and employment figures.
Content:
Markets Weekly Chartstopper: February 27, 2026

February 27, 2026 — 04:50 pm EST

Artificial intelligence (AI) fears may have reached new heights this week, with some attributing Monday’s selloff to a futuristic viral blog post. But, in the here and now, there was some more positive AI news: Anthropic’s update on its enterprise AI agents actually boosted some software stocks since it includes plugins for some of the very same software companies caught up in the months-long AI-related selloff.

NVIDIA’s fourth-quarter earnings highlighted continued AI chip demand, nearly doubling profits year on year and projecting higher first-quarter revenue ($78 billion) than expected. And yet, with investors questioning the durability of demand, the stock was down over 5% the next day.

So, between concerns about AI’s economic impact and geopolitical tensions, there’s been a flight to safety this week, pushing down 10-year Treasury yields over 10 basis points to approximately 3.95%, while the Nasdaq-100® ended the week flat.

Title: Getting Started With The Popover API
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Godstime Aburu)
Summary: The article explains how building effective, accessible tooltips was historically complex, relying on JavaScript libraries to manage interactions and accessibility. The new Popover API, supported by most browsers, offers a native solution that simplifies implementation, enhances accessibility, and reduces maintenance, representing a significant advancement over traditional, library-dependent methods.
Content:
A tooltip may seem like a small UI element, but building one that works well across all users and devices can be surprisingly complex. Traditionally, developers relied on JavaScript libraries to implement tooltips, as browsers did not have a native, standardized way to handle them comprehensively. This often led to issues such as flickering, overlapping content, inconsistent accessibility support, and complicated event handling.

Over time, the complexity of managing tooltip behavior—handling hover, focus, outside clicks, ARIA attributes, and keyboard interactions—caused codebases to become unwieldy. Developers would add layers of logic to fix bugs, which made maintenance difficult. Libraries helped, but they often acted as black boxes, obscuring how the underlying interactions worked.

Recently, the introduction of the Popover API offers a new approach. Instead of relying on external libraries, developers can now use the browser’s native support to create accessible, reliable tooltips. This API handles opening, closing, keyboard interaction, and accessibility features at the platform level, reducing the need for custom JavaScript and complex event management.

While the Popover API is supported by most browsers, some parts are still evolving. Developers are encouraged to monitor browser compatibility and updates. Using this native API can simplify tooltip implementation, improve accessibility, and reduce maintenance overhead, marking a significant step forward from traditional, library-dependent methods.

Title: February 2026 Review and Outlook
Author: The Market Intelligence Desk Team
Summary: In February 2026, U.S. equities showed mixed results amid sector rotation. Large-cap indices were restrained by declines in mega-cap growth, especially AI-related stocks, while equal-weight, mid-cap, and value sectors outperformed. Treasury yields fell, and energy prices rose amid geopolitical risks. Market breadth improved, with broader leadership emerging, reflecting confidence in economic resilience despite ongoing dispersion beneath the surface.
Content:
US Markets February 2026 Review and Outlook

The Market Intelligence Desk Team

Market Intelligence Desk by this author ->

Executive Summary

U.S. equities delivered a mixed performance amid significant internal rotation. Market leadership broadened as equal-weight, mid-cap, and value exposures outperformed. Market breadth improved, with seven of 11 large-cap sectors within 0.5% of their respective 52-week highs. Mega-cap growth and AI-adjacent software weighed on cap-weighted indices. Treasury yields declined meaningfully, led by the long end. Energy prices strengthened amid geopolitical risk, reinforcing real-asset leadership. Earnings growth remained solid despite increasing dispersion beneath the surface.

U.S. equity markets delivered a mixed performance in February, with headline indices masking significant rotation and dispersion beneath the surface. Among large caps, the Dow Jones Industrials rose modestly by 0.3%, the S&P 500 declined 0.8% for the second time in three months, and the Nasdaq 100 fell 2.3%, marking its worst monthly decline since March 2025. Cap-weighted indices were restrained by continued corrective price action in select large-cap growth stocks, as evidenced by the Magnificent Seven Index, which declined 7.3%, its worst since March 2025. Conversely, the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index gained 3.5%, its best since May 2025, outperforming the cap-weighted S&P 500 for the fourth consecutive month. The S&P Midcap 400 rose 4.1%, its best month since May 2025.

Importantly, February did not resemble a broad risk-off environment. Instead, market action was characterized by rotation rather than liquidation. Investors continued reallocating away from concentrated leadership toward a broader mix of cyclicals and defensives, reflected in strong gains across energy, utilities, materials, staples, industrials, and healthcare. This shift suggests confidence in the durability of economic activity, even as debate persists about the return profile and timing of large-scale AI investments.

From a macro perspective, the backdrop remained supportive but nuanced. Economic data indicated a gradually cooling economy rather than contraction, while inflation progress remained uneven. Treasury yields declined significantly during the month, with the 10-year yield falling below 4%, reinforcing expectations for a patient Federal Reserve. Overall, February appeared less like a turning point for equities and more an extension of an ongoing transition toward broader participation and more selective leadership.

The divergence in February was largely driven by continued weakness in mega-cap growth, particularly within the technology sector.

Title: Armed robots take to the battlefield in Ukraine war
Author: Unknown
Summary: Since Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the conflict has become high-tech, with drones and uncrewed boats. Ukraine has deployed armed ground robots (UGVs) for combat, successfully repelling attacks and capturing prisoners. These semi-autonomous robots, controlled remotely, are armed with weapons and used for attack, defense, and logistics, with their role expected to expand in future warfare.
Content:
Since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion, the war in Ukraine has developed into a high-tech conflict. Swarms of spy and killer drones have set the skies of Ukraine abuzz, and uncrewed boats have crippled the Russian navy in the Black Sea. Now, Ukraine has embarked on a massive program to deploy armed robots on the ground.

Uncrewed ground vehicles (UGVs), or ground robot systems as they are known in Ukrainian military parlance, have already proven their worth. There have been reports of UGVs successfully repelling Russian attacks and even taking enemy soldiers prisoner. Ukrainian and Russian killer robots are even said to have clashed without humans being present at the site of the battle.

"Robot wars are already happening," said Oleksandr Afanasiev from the Ukrainian army's K2 brigade, which he commands as its UGV battalion—the world's first, he claims. The brigade has been mounting Kalashnikov machine guns on top of these robots, which "open fire on a battlefield where an infantryman would be afraid to turn up. But a UGV is happy to risk its existence," Afanasiev explained.

His battalion has also been using explosive-laden, battery-powered kamikaze UGVs to blow up enemy positions and hideouts. Unlike aerial drones that buzz overhead, these make no sound to warn the enemy of an impending strike. The deputy commander of the 33rd Detached Mechanised Brigade's tank battalion, known by the callsign Afghan, reported that one Ukrainian UGV armed with a machine gun ambushed a Russian personnel carrier, while another robot defended a Ukrainian position for weeks.

Afghan acknowledged there are limits to the killer robots' autonomy on the battlefield, citing ethical and international humanitarian law considerations. "Modern UGVs are part-autonomous. They can move on their own, observe, and detect the enemy. But still, the decision to open fire is made by a human, their operator," he said. "Robots can misidentify the wrong person or attack a civilian. That's why the final decision must be made by an operator."

Most armed UGVs are remotely controlled by operators over the internet from a safe distance. Ukraine's lethal UGVs can be armed with grenade launchers as well as machine guns, and can also be used to plant landmines or barbed wire. However, the majority of uncrewed vehicles are still used for their original purposes of delivering supplies and evacuating the wounded.

According to Valerii Zaluzhnyi, Ukraine's former commander-in-chief and now ambassador to the UK, the role of armed UGVs is expected to grow exponentially. Speaking at the London think tank Chatham House about the future of warfare, he described how strike UGVs would be utilized in upcoming conflicts.

Title: One Suspect Charged for Foolio's Murder Wants Separate Trial
Author: Trent Fitzgerald
Summary: Rashad Murphy, charged with Foolio's 2024 murder, seeks a separate trial or jury, citing potential prejudice from evidence involving co-defendants. Murphy's attorneys filed a supplemental memorandum addressing this issue. Four other suspects, including Alicia Andrews, are also charged; Andrews was convicted of manslaughter, with sentencing delayed. The suspects' trial proceedings continue in Florida.
Content:
One of the suspects charged with Foolio's murder is to have his own trial. According to documents obtained by XXL, attorneys for Rashad Murphy, one of the suspects in the 2024 murder of rapper Foolio, filed a supplemental memorandum on Thursday (March 5) requesting to be tried separately from his co-defendants for their upcoming trial, or at least have a different jury. Last month, the court held a hearing to address the motion but no ruling was made.

On Friday (March 6), Murphy's attorneys filed an amended memorandum addressing the court's indication that evidence concerning a separate shooting involving Murphy's co-defendants might be admissible against all defendants. This would happen if the judge determines that the evidence is "inextricably intertwined" with the charges related to Foolio's killing. 

Florida law requires a defendant-specific relevance and prejudice analysis in joint trials. Rashad Murphy respectfully submits this supplemental memorandum to address that issue.

Rashad Murphy is one of five people charged with Foolio's murder, along with Alicia Andrews, Isaiah Chance, Sean Gathright, and Davion Murphy. The Jacksonville, Fla., rapper was shot and killed by three masked gunmen while celebrating his birthday in Tampa, Fla., on June 23, 2004.

In October 2025, Andrews was convicted of manslaughter in connection with Foolio's killing and faces a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison. Her sentencing was delayed after the original judge was removed in January by an appellate court due to bias. Andrews’ new sentencing hearing is now scheduled for December 8.

Meanwhile, the four other murder suspects—Rashad, Davion, Isaiah, and Sean Gathright—were in court on Wednesday (March 4), where a judge denied several motions, including some Florida statutes, the death penalty, and victim impact evidence. Video of their court hearing can be viewed below.

Title: Trump demands 'unconditional surrender' from Iran as Putin speaks with Iran's president
Author: Unknown
Summary: US President Donald Trump demanded Iran's "unconditional surrender" amid ongoing US and Israeli strikes, asserting Iran's future depends on acceptable leadership. Trump emphasized no deal unless Iran surrenders and suggested he may influence Iran's next leader, opposing radical or extremist figures. The White House expects the conflict to last four to six weeks, with no regime change explicitly called for.
Content:
US President Donald Trump has demanded Iran's "unconditional surrender" as the American and Israeli military continued to launch strikes. Trump said the US and allies would make Iran "economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before" after it picked "acceptable" new leadership.

With the war close to entering a second week, it remains unclear who will lead Iran after the killing of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei a week ago, or on what terms the US would end its offensive against Tehran, which has triggered region-wide retaliatory strikes. Speaking on Friday, White House spokeswoman Karoline Leavitt said the administration expected military operations to last four to six weeks.

Writing on his Truth Social platform, Trump said: "There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER! After that, and the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of our wonderful and very brave allies and partners, will work tirelessly to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction, making it economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before. IRAN WILL HAVE A GREAT FUTURE. MAKE IRAN GREAT AGAIN (MIGA!)."

Trump's latest intervention suggested he was not interested in a negotiated settlement to the war, though he stopped short of calling for regime change. Since the beginning of the conflict, Trump has avoided the term "regime change," a sign he may be wary of drawing parallels between the attack on Iran and past US wars in the Middle East that he criticised before entering politics.

Other senior administration officials have made that argument on Trump's behalf in recent days, saying the president will not let the US get drawn into a protracted, open-ended conflict like in Iraq and Afghanistan. Asked about the duration of the offensive, Leavitt told reporters: "We expect [the war] to last about 4 to 6 weeks, and we are well on our way to achieving those objectives, annihilating Iran's navy."

She also said Trump did not want Iran to be led by "a radical terrorist or a regime that chants death to America." The White House press secretary told reporters: "[Trump] wants to take an interest in and pursue who the next leader of the Iranian country is going to be. And that has yet to be decided."

Earlier this week, Trump told US outlet Axios he expected to play a role in the selection of Iran's next leader. Responding to reports that Khamenei's son Mojtaba—a conservative close to Iran's powerful Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps—could be installed as leader, Trump said that would be "unacceptable." Trump has likened the situation in Iran to the US raid on Venezuela earlier this year.

Title: Robinhood’s startup fund stumbles in NYSE debut
Author: Marina Temkin
Summary: Robinhood launched Robinhood Ventures Fund I to allow retail investors to access private startups like Databricks, Stripe, and Revolut. The fund raised $658.4 million of its $705.7 million target, but shares declined 16% on debut. Unlike Destiny Tech100, which trades above NAV, Robinhood's fund lacks exposure to high-profile companies like OpenAI and SpaceX. Robinhood plans to add more late-stage startups.
Content:
Retail investors are often excluded from the startup world. Robinhood is attempting to change that by allowing the public to invest in a portfolio of what it calls “some of the most exciting private companies operating today.” To do this, the company has secured access to eight startups—including Databricks, Stripe, Mercor, and Oura—grouped into a vehicle called Robinhood Ventures Fund I. The fund also includes Ramp, Airwallex, Revolut, and Boom. It launched last month with an ambitious $1 billion target, but demand was lower than expected.

On Thursday, Robinhood announced the fund had raised $658.4 million, which could reach $705.7 million if underwriters exercise their full allotment. The shares, priced at $25 in the offering, began trading on Friday and closed the day at $21, a 16% decline.

The reception of Robinhood Ventures (RVI) on Wall Street contrasts sharply with another effort to give individual investors exposure to buzzy startups. When Destiny Tech100, a publicly traded, closed-end fund holding stakes in 100 venture-backed companies including SpaceX, OpenAI, and Discord, direct-listed on the NYSE in March 2024, its shares surged from a reference price of $4.84 to an opening trade of $8.25, eventually closing its first day at $9.00. Since then, Destiny Tech100 has continued to climb, closing trading on Friday at $26.61, a 33% premium to its net asset value of $19.97, meaning its shares trade well above the value of its underlying holdings.

The key difference appears to be that Robinhood’s fund lacks exposure to companies widely expected to go public at enormous valuations, such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX. Robinhood aims to address this by adding more startups to the fund, eventually targeting “15 to 20 of the best late-stage growth companies,” according to Robinhood Ventures President Sarah Pinto. The company’s CFO, Shiv Verma, told Axios that Robinhood is also looking to gain exposure to OpenAI.

Title: Marathon interactive maps
Author: Polygon Staff
Summary: The article introduces interactive maps for the game Marathon's launch zones: Perimeter, Dire Marsh, and Outpost, with Cryo Archive coming later. Perimeter is beginner-friendly but challenging, featuring hotspots like Hauler and tunnels. Dire Marsh is swampy with stronger enemies, while Outpost is a tough, close-quarters UESC facility. These maps help players locate locked rooms, exfil points, and targets.
Content:
No Ads Ninja v10 Client!

Jump Links Perimeter interactive map Dire Marsh interactive map Outpost interactive map Whether you're looking for locked rooms, exfil locations, or priority targets, our Marathon interactive maps are what you need. There are two zones in Marathon at launch — Perimeter and Dire Marsh — and each of them offers a different environment to hunt down other runners and UESC enemies alike, before getting the heck out of there with your hard-earned loot. Here are our interactive Marathon maps for Perimeter, Dire Marsh, and Outpost. Expect an interactive map for the game's hardest map, Cryo Archive, when it becomes playable later this month.

Perimeter interactive map Considered the best map for beginners, don't let that fool you. Perimeter can still give you a tough time. Hauler is one of the most active player hotspots here, as one of the only ways from the southern half of the map to the north, due to the Data Wall that stretches horizontally. Then there's Tunnels, which is a mazy network of underground tunnels that houses plenty of high-tier loot. Not to mention Station and Overflow, which you must complete the "Survival Directive" priority contract.

Dire Marsh interactive map Dire Marsh is a swampy environment, with Algae Ponds being the most populated; some players have referred to it as "Marathon's Tilted Towers." You can complete "Introducing: Traxus" at Intersection, Bio-Research, or Complex, and there's also the Maintenance Pump key you can use in the southwest corner. The UESC threats on Dire Marsh are stronger than Perimeter, so make sure you're using the best weapons and classes.

Outpost interactive map Outpost is a close-quarters map that takes place within a massive UESC facility. The enemies are tougher, extraction is more difficult, and even the air itself will sometimes combust into hellfire. This map is brutal for new runners. Bring some good equipment before you tackle this map.

Title: Surfers in Munich (Yes, Munich) Just Want Their Wave Back
Author: Christopher F. Schuetze and Laetitia Vançon
Summary: Two men in Munich attempted to recreate the city’s artificial wave in the Eisbach creek, which was disrupted after dredging last year. Authorities now require a safer, fixed wave maker, but efforts to install one have failed due to strong currents dislodging the equipment. The local surfing community is working to restore the popular surf spot amid safety concerns.
Content:
Ignoring the winter freeze from the Alps, two men shed their parkas one recent morning, squirmed into wet suits, and walked into a raging creek in downtown Munich. A helper handed them a 20-foot metal bar, which the men shoved underwater. They were trying to create a wave by installing an obstacle that would bend the churning water to surfable perfection. And they were failing.

Munich, despite sitting some 200 miles from the nearest sea, was an unlikely surf spot until recently. Enthusiasts flocked to the waterway, called the Eisbach, in a park near a university neighborhood, to take turns riding the artificial wave. But after the authorities dredged the channel last year, the wave vanished.

Now the Munich surfing community — yes, there is such a thing — is trying to remake it. Nature and bureaucracy have complicated things. The city authorities have urged caution after a death at the creek last year. They want the surfers to install a safer kind of wave maker — one that is fixed to the creek bed, unlike the old version, which hung from a bridge.

The problem is that the surfers’ efforts to satisfy that demand have not yet made waves: When the men in wet suits jammed the metal bar into the creek bed, the Eisbach’s current kept dislodging it.

Title: For Xi, Trump’s Embrace of War Proves China Needs More Power
Author: David Pierson
Summary: U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran, including the killing of Iran’s supreme leader, reinforce Xi Jinping’s view that hard power is essential. Xi has prioritized building a strong military to deter U.S. aggression amid concerns over American force disrupting global order. While China believes it’s less likely to be targeted due to its nuclear status, Beijing sees U.S. military actions as a persistent threat.
Content:
The sudden and furious attacks by U.S. and Israeli forces on Iran this past week, including the killing of the country’s supreme leader, are confirming Xi Jinping’s worldview that hard power is king.

For years, Mr. Xi, China’s top leader, has warned his country about American military hostility and directed his generals to build a world-class army, or what he called a “Great Wall of Steel,” strong enough to deter the United States and ensure peace on Beijing’s terms. “It is necessary to speak to invaders in the language they know,” Mr. Xi said. “That is, a war must be fought to deter invasion, and a victory is needed to win peace and respect.”

Mr. Xi’s more than decade-long pursuit of the power to fight fire with fire is now given added urgency because of President Trump’s unapologetic use of military force, which is threatening governments and disrupting a global order that China increasingly sees itself as eventually leading.

While Mr. Xi and Mr. Trump are set to meet in several weeks in Beijing to extend a fragile truce after a punishing trade war last year, the specter of brute American force taking down sovereign leaders like Iran’s Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and capturing others like President Nicolás Maduro of Venezuela remains unsettling for China. Both Ayatollah Khamenei and Mr. Maduro were strategic partners to Beijing.

Chinese analysts and officials do not believe the United States would target Mr. Xi in the same way, given China’s status as a nuclear power. But Mr. Trump’s embrace of war as a tool to assert American dominance has nonetheless reaffirmed Beijing’s view that the United States remains China’s most enduring threat.

Title: We've been speaking to Iranians during one week of war. Here's what they said
Author: Unknown
Summary: During a week of war, Iranians expressed mixed emotions—some celebrated the death of Supreme Leader Khamenei and cheered airstrikes, hoping regime change, while others felt fear, uncertainty, and skepticism about the war’s motives. Despite internet restrictions and repression, BBC Persian reports a population divided between hope for regime collapse and concern over ongoing violence and geopolitical interests.
Content:
We've been speaking to Iranians during one week of war. Here's what they said.

On Monday, three days into the war, men in Tehran watched from a hillside as a plume of smoke rose after an explosion.

When Hamid heard news of the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei a week ago, he felt a wave of euphoria and took his wife and daughter into the street outside his home in Tehran to celebrate. For the next few days, as US and Israeli bombs slammed into buildings across the capital, the family went onto the roof of the house to watch the airstrikes coming in, cheering every time a regime target was hit. 

"Try to find anywhere else on this earth where the population would be happy with an external attack on their country," he told me, via a cousin in the UK. "But we now have hope that the regime will soon be gone. We are happy."

Hamid—not his real name—is not alone. Alongside colleagues from BBC Persian, we have been hearing from people inside and outside Iran on a momentous week for them, for the future of their country, and for the entire region. BBC Persian is the Persian language service of BBC News, used by 24 million people around the world—the majority in Iran—despite being blocked and routinely jammed by Iranian authorities.

In a police state under bombardment, with severe internet restrictions in place, it is impossible to fully gauge the mood across a vast nation of 90 million people. Residents of Tehran have received messages warning: "If your connection to the internet continues in the coming days, your line will be blocked and you will be referred to judicial authorities."

The regime still instills fear, and no one who voices opposition to it is willing to give their name for fear of repercussions on themselves or their families. But a week on, while some are still celebrating every strike on the regime, others are increasingly frightened, questioning the motives and endgame of the war.

"The goal of this war isn't to bring about freedom or democracy for the Iranian people," Ali told us. "It's for the geopolitical benefit of Israel, the US, and Arab countries in the region."

Mohammad, who is in his 30s and lives in Tehran, said he had wanted a deal between the US and Iran that would have avoided the war. "Deep down, I always hoped an agreement would be made," he said. He had thought he would be happy at Khamenei's death, but in the end "felt nothing." He told my colleague Soroush Pakzad that he is now filled with uncertainty about the future—and, with regime checkpoints everywhere on the ground and bombardments from the sky, he is afraid.

Others Iranians speak of feeling a mix of fear, stress, and hope. One woman told me that it would take living in Iran for 40 years to understand the complexity of what she and other Iranians are now feeling. "We laugh and are happy when the regime is..."

Title: New Airstrikes Batter Tehran
Author: The New York Times
Summary: The Israeli military launched widespread strikes on Iranian infrastructure, including near Mehrabad Airport in Tehran, damaging planes. Iran responded with drone and missile attacks on Israel. The conflict has expanded, with reports of Russian intelligence sharing with Iran, and ongoing bombings in Lebanon causing mass displacement. Oil prices surged amid the escalating Middle East conflict.
Content:
The Israeli military said it had launched “a broad-scale wave of strikes” on Iranian government infrastructure in the early hours of Saturday morning. Soon after, Iranian state media reported that Israeli strikes had hit the area near Mehrabad Airport in Tehran. Two residents of Tehran who live near the airport said in text messages that it appeared to have been badly damaged and that it looked like commercial planes were burning on the tarmac. The Israeli military did not immediately respond to questions about whether it had struck the airport.

The new strikes in Tehran came hours after it emerged that U.S. officials believe Russia has provided intelligence to Iran during the U.S.-Israeli war, including satellite imagery showing the locations of warships and military personnel. The involvement of Russia was the latest indication that the Middle East conflict was expanding. The information sharing by Russia could further complicate relations between Washington and Moscow, even though some officials downplayed its importance, noting that Russia has long provided similar information to Iran and it was unclear if Tehran could act on it.

Bombing in Iran and Lebanon continued unabated on Friday, as oil and gas prices surged again, reflecting how the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran, now nearly a week old, was affecting the global economy. President Trump demanded “unconditional surrender” by Iran on Friday via social media, marking the most uncompromising stance he has taken so far and suggesting a potential for a prolonged conflict in the Middle East.

On Friday afternoon, Israeli officials announced that their forces had destroyed an underground bunker used by Iran’s supreme leader before he was killed last week. The Israeli military also targeted the southern outskirts of Beirut and issued additional evacuation warnings in Lebanon as it intensified its campaign against Iran-backed Hezbollah militants. An estimated 300,000 people in Lebanon have fled their homes since the bombing began, according to the Norwegian Refugee Council.

Mohamed Hjoula, 35, who had taken refuge with about 40 family members on Beirut’s waterfront promenade after leaving their homes, said, “We civilians are paying the price of war.”

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps launched a wave of drones and missiles at Israel, according to a statement from the force reported by IRNA, Iran’s state news agency. Air-raid sirens sounded in Tel Aviv, and the Israeli military said it detected missile launches from Iran, though there were no immediate reports of major damage.

The Israeli military reported striking more than 400 targets in western Iran on Friday, including missile launchers and drone storage sites.

Oil and gasoline prices surged, with the U.S. benchmark crude rising sharply, reflecting the economic impact of the ongoing conflict.

Title: Conflict spreads across the Middle East in days after U.S.-Israel attack kills Iran's supreme leader
Author: Sarah Petz
Summary: Conflict in the Middle East escalates after the U.S.-Israel attack kills Iran's supreme leader. Iran retaliates with strikes on Israel and U.S. bases, while Israel targets Hezbollah in Lebanon. Hundreds are dead, thousands evacuated, and a humanitarian crisis ensues. U.S. President Trump calls for "unconditional surrender," and Canada evacuates 3,500 citizens. Iran reports over 1,230 deaths; Lebanon reports 217 deaths.
Content:
Conflict spreads across the Middle East days after the U.S.-Israel attack kills Iran's supreme leader. Countries rush to evacuate citizens as Iran retaliates for ongoing strikes. Rescuers inspect the site of an Israel and U.S. strike on a police station in Tehran, Iran, on March 3.

The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran continues to widen after nearly a week, leaving hundreds dead, thousands seeking evacuation, and many facing a humanitarian crisis. Iran launched retaliatory attacks on Israel and U.S. bases, while Israel intensified its campaign against Iran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon.

On Friday, U.S. President Donald Trump stated that the only deal with Iran to potentially end the war would be "unconditional surrender." Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada had no plans to engage militarily as of Friday, focusing instead on evacuating 3,500 Canadians who requested assistance from the region.

The death tolls as of Friday include more than 1,230 people in Iran. The Lebanese Health Ministry reported 217 people killed and more than 790 wounded due to Israeli attacks.

Title: Fresh Energy In March (2026 Wallpapers Edition)
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Cosima Mielke)
Summary: March's wallpaper, designed by Ginger from Serbia, features a blooming flower symbolizing renewal. Available in various resolutions, it can be downloaded for free to refresh desktops and celebrate spring's arrival.
Content: With spring approaching and other seasons vying for attention, March greets us with blossoms. Designed by Ginger from Serbia, this wallpaper features a delicate, blooming flower symbolizing renewal and new beginnings. The collection offers various screen resolutions and is available for free download, allowing users to refresh their desktops and find inspiration for the season ahead.

Title: Kit: An Opinionated API Starter Kit for Laravel
Author: Yannick Lyn Fatt
Summary: Kit by Steve Mc Dougallis is a Laravel API starter kit featuring token-based authentication, API documentation, and security defaults. It supports versioned JSON APIs, invokable controllers, form request validation, and JSON:API responses. Includes auth routes, security measures like rate limiting and headers, sunset middleware for deprecations, and localization. Setup involves cloning, dependencies, and running migrations; documentation is auto-generated with Scribe.
Content:
Kit by Steve Mc Dougallis is a Laravel API starter kit built around token-based authentication, API documentation, and security-first defaults. It targets developers who want a structured starting point for building versioned JSON APIs without wiring everything up from scratch.

Getting Started

Clone the repository, install dependencies, and run the setup script:

```bash
composer install
composer run setup
php artisan serve
```

The `composer run setup` command copies `.env.example` to `.env`, generates an app key, and runs migrations against a local SQLite database.

API Architecture

The kit takes a few deliberate architectural positions:

- No global or API prefix—routes are versioned directly, e.g., `/v1/auth/login`.
- Invokable controllers only—each controller has a single `__invoke` method.
- Form Requests for validation—request payloads are validated in dedicated Form Request classes, with DTO-style payload classes in `app/Http/Payloads/V1`.
- JSON:API resource format—responses follow a consistent structure for entity data.

Authentication

Nine routes handle the full auth lifecycle:

- Registration and login (both return tokens via Laravel Sanctum).
- `/v1/auth/me` (requires Bearer token).
- Email verification via signed URLs.
- Password reset with anti-enumeration responses (the same response is returned whether or not the email exists).

Security Defaults

The kit includes several security defaults out of the box:

- ULID primary keys for user records.
- Predefined rate limits on auth endpoints, configurable in the App Service Provider.
- Mandatory `Content-Type: application/json` enforcement on write requests.
- Hardened response headers, including `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, and `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`.
- Request ID tracking.
- Audit logging for sensitive operations.

Sunset Middleware

The kit also includes a Sunset middleware for deprecating API endpoints over time. It is applied directly to a route with three parameters: the sunset date, a successor URL, and a boolean to control enforcement:

```php
Route::middleware('sunset:2027-01-01,https://api.acme.com/v2/auth/login,true')->post('/v1/auth/login', LoginController::class);
```

While the endpoint is active, the middleware appends `Deprecation`, `Sunset`, and `Link` (successor version) headers to responses, allowing API clients to detect deprecation and plan accordingly. After the sunset date and enforcement, the endpoint returns a 410 Gone status.

Documentation

API documentation is generated using Scribe. Annotations are attribute-based rather than docblock-based, and the setup produces an OpenAPI specification alongside the docs.

Localization

The kit respects the `Accept-Language` request header and responds with a `Content-Language` header. Supported locales default to English, with translation files at `lang/en/api.php` and `lang/es/api.php`.

Tooling

The project uses Pest for testing, PHPStan for static analysis, Pint for code formatting, and Rector for automated refactoring. GitHub Actions workflows support continuous integration.

Title: Ward: A Security Scanner for Laravel
Author: Yannick Lyn Fatt
Summary: Ward is a Go-based command-line security scanner tailored for Laravel projects. It analyzes project structure—routes, models, controllers, configs, dependencies—and performs targeted security checks across four engines: environment variables, configuration files, dependencies, and custom rules. It offers multiple output formats (JSON, SARIF, HTML, Markdown) and integrates with CI/CD pipelines, providing detailed, sortable reports to identify vulnerabilities and misconfigurations.
Content:
Ward: A Security Scanner for Laravel

Last updated on March 1st, 2026 by Yannick Lyn Fatt

Ward, created by El Jakani Yassine, is a command-line security scanner written in Go designed around Laravel's structure. Rather than running generic pattern matching across your codebase, it first parses your project's structure—routes, models, controllers, middleware, Blade templates, config files, environment variables, and dependencies—then runs targeted checks against that context.

Installation

Ward is distributed as a Go binary, so you'll need to ensure you have Go installed. You can then run:

```bash
go install github.com/eljakani/ward@latest
```

Make sure `$GOPATH/bin` is in your PATH:

```bash
export PATH="$PATH:$(go env GOPATH)/bin"
```

After installing, run:

```bash
ward init
```

to create `~/.ward/` with a default config file, 42+ built-in rules organized by category, and directories for reports and scan history.

Scanning a Project

Point Ward at a local directory or a remote Git repository:

- Local project:

```bash
ward scan /path/to/laravel-project
```

- Remote repository (shallow cloned):

```bash
ward scan https://github.com/user/laravel-project.git
```

When run in a terminal, Ward displays a TUI. A scan view shows pipeline progress and live severity counts as scanners run. Once complete, a results view presents a sortable findings table with severity badges, category grouping, and a detail panel showing descriptions, code snippets, and remediation guidance.

What It Checks

Ward ships with four independent scan engines:

- **env-scanner:** Runs 8 checks against your `.env` file, including debug mode enabled in production, missing or weak `APP_KEY`, and secrets leaked in `.env.example`.

- **config-scanner:** Runs 13 checks across your `config/*.php` files, covering hardcoded credentials, insecure session flags, CORS wildcard origins, and missing security options.

- **dependency-scanner:** Queries the OSV.dev advisory database in real time against your `composer.lock` to find vulnerable Packagist packages. Because it queries live data rather than a bundled list, it reflects current advisories.

- **rules-scanner:** Applies 42 rules across 7 categories: secrets (hardcoded passwords, API keys, AWS credentials), injection (SQL, command, eval), XSS (unescaped Blade output, JavaScript injection), debug artifacts (`dd()`, `dump()`, `phpinfo()`), weak cryptography (MD5, SHA1, insecure RNG), configuration issues (CORS, CSRF, mass assignment), and authentication gaps (missing middleware, absent rate limiting).

Output Formats

Configure output formats in `~/.ward/config.yaml`:

```yaml
output:
  formats: [json, sarif, html, markdown]
  dir: ./reports
```

- **JSON:** Machine-readable results

- **SARIF:** Compatible with GitHub Code Scanning and IDE integrations

- **HTML:** Standalone dark-themed visual report

- **Markdown:** Suitable for PR comments

CI/CD Integration

Ward returns non-zero exit codes when findings meet or exceed a specified severity, making it straightforward to gate deployments:

```bash
ward scan --output json --fail-on high
```

Title: Polyscope Is an Ai-First Dev Environment for Orchestrating Agents
Author: Paul Redmond
Summary: Beyond Code has launched Polyscope, a free AI-first development environment for orchestrating multiple AI agents simultaneously. It features fast copy-on-write cloning, a built-in preview browser, multi-model querying, and workspace integration. Available for macOS, Polyscope offers free and paid plans for individual developers and teams.
Content:
Polyscope Is an AI-First Dev Environment for Orchestrating Agents

The team at Beyond Code has announced Polyscope, a free agent orchestration tool designed to run multiple AI agents simultaneously.

From the announcement: "Today we're announcing Polyscope - the free agent orchestration tool of my dreams. Run dozens of AI agents at the same time, blazing fast copy-on-write clones, a built-in preview browser you can use to visually prompt your agents, and much more."

Key features include:
- Running dozens of AI agents in parallel
- Copy-on-write clones for fast agent branching
- Built-in preview browser for visually prompting agents
- Querying multiple models to collaborate on a question
- Connecting workspaces to multiple repositories

Polyscope is available for macOS at getpolyscope.com, offering a free plan and plans for solo developers and teams.

Title: Hit & Run / Assault - 400 Block Witney Avenue North
Author: Unknown
Summary: Police are investigating a vehicle chase and assault on Witney Avenue North on March 5, 2026. A civilian vehicle chased and struck another, leading to an occupant assaulting the driver. The victim sustained non-life-threatening injuries; the suspect vehicle fled. The incident followed a confrontation near 22nd Street West and Idylwyld Drive North. Both parties are believed to know each other.
Content:
Police are investigating following a civilian vehicle chase that led to an assault. 

Shortly before 10:00 a.m. on March 5, 2026, police were called to the 400 block of Witney Avenue North following multiple reports of altercations between the occupants of two vehicles. Information was received that a civilian vehicle had been chasing another before intentionally striking it in the 400 block of Witney Avenue. An occupant then exited the vehicle and began assaulting the driver of the vehicle that had been struck. 

Upon arrival, officers located the victim of the assault suffering from non-life-threatening injuries; the other involved vehicle fled prior to police arrival. 

Through investigation, officers learned that a confrontational exchange took place in the area of 22nd Street West and Idylwyld Drive North between two parties before one began chasing the other in their vehicles. During the chase, multiple uninvolved parked vehicles were struck and damaged. 

Both parties are believed to have been known to one another, and police are continuing to investigate.

Title: Justice Department publishes documents with sexual assault allegations against Trump
Author: Erica Orden
Summary: The Justice Department released FBI interviews of a woman alleging that President Trump sexually assaulted her as a teenager after meeting him through Jeffrey Epstein. The woman claimed Trump hit her after she bit him during an attempted sexual assault. Trump denies the allegations, which lack corroboration. The files, released amid political scrutiny, include unsubstantiated claims from the woman.
Content:
The Justice Department posted a series of FBI interviews with a woman who alleged that President Donald Trump sexually assaulted her when she was a young teenager after being introduced to him by Jeffrey Epstein. According to FBI summaries of her interviews, known as FBI 302s, her central allegation is that Trump hit her after she bit his penis when he attempted to force her to perform oral sex.

The three files were released amid investigations by Democrats into whether the department purposefully withheld materials that included sexual assault allegations against Trump. Trump has denied any wrongdoing related to the Epstein allegations and has not been charged with a crime in connection with them. There is no evidence suggesting Trump participated in Epstein’s sex trafficking operation. Many of the materials released by the Justice Department lack substantiation or context.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt dismissed the allegations as “completely baseless accusations, backed by zero credible evidence, from a sadly disturbed woman who has an extensive criminal history.” She also claimed that the Department of Justice knew about these allegations for four years but took no action, asserting that Trump has been exonerated by the release of the Epstein files.

The files, dated between August and October 2019, describe that when the woman was between 13 and 15 years old, Epstein took her to either New York or New Jersey, where she was introduced to Trump in a large building. She stated that Trump “didn’t like that I was a boy-girl,” which was interpreted as her being a tomboy. She recalled Trump asking others to leave the room, then saying, “Let me teach you how little girls are supposed to be,” before unzipping his pants and instructing her to put her head “down to his penis.” She said she bit him during the incident, after which he pulled her hair and punched her on the side of her head. She recalled him saying, “Get this little bitch the hell out of here,” as others reentered the room. The FBI interviews did not specify how the incident ended or how she exited the encounter.

In one interview, the woman disclosed that she had begun working with attorneys and wanted to be upfront about her pending civil case.

Title: A Tale of Two Earnings Cycles
Author: Phil Mackintosh
Summary: In Q4 2025, both Nasdaq-100® large caps and small caps experienced strong earnings growth, with Nasdaq posting 17% year-over-year gains and small caps returning to positive growth after 11 quarters. Nasdaq's growth is driven by higher margins and less sensitivity to economic headwinds, while small caps benefited from easing inflation, wage pressures, and declining interest costs.
Content:
Earnings: A Tale of Two Earnings Cycles

The fourth quarter of 2025 continued a recent trend: the Nasdaq-100® large caps and S&P 600 small caps both experienced strong earnings growth. However, the drivers behind these trends are quite different.

Both large and small caps are seeing strong earnings growth now. The Nasdaq-100® posted 17% year-over-year earnings growth in Q4 2025, extending its streak to 11 consecutive quarters of at least 15% growth. In contrast, small caps had endured 11 straight quarters of negative earnings growth from Q3 2022 through Q1 2025. But that trend reversed in Q2 2025, and Q4 marked its third consecutive quarter of positive earnings growth.

Looking ahead, consensus estimates project the Nasdaq-100® to continue its streak of 15%+ earnings growth over the next year, while small caps are expected to maintain their positive growth trend. Despite both indexes showing strong earnings growth today, the underlying drivers differ significantly.

The Nasdaq-100® has been less sensitive to headwinds that drove small caps into recession. Earnings growth can be broken down into three components: earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT), interest costs, and taxes and other items. Using last twelve months (LTM) data, the comparison reveals notable differences.

Small caps faced multiple headwinds over the past couple of years. They experienced negative EBIT growth due to margin pressures from high inflation and wage increases, along with less pricing power compared to large caps. Rising interest costs, driven by Federal Reserve rate hikes from 2022 to 2024, also weighed on small caps. Recently, as inflation and wages slowed, EBIT growth turned positive, aided by rate cuts that stabilized interest costs, which are expected to decline further.

In contrast, the Nasdaq-100® appeared to operate in a world less affected by these headwinds. While these challenges impacted large caps less—thanks to higher margins—they still played a role in their earnings dynamics.

Title: Traffic Collison - Circle Dr E/Hwy 11 North Bound Lanes
Author: Unknown
Summary: Saskatoon police report that one eastbound lane on Circle Drive and one northbound lane on Highway 11 have reopened after a semi carrying equipment damaged the Highway 11 overpass. No injuries occurred. Traffic restrictions remain, with engineers assessing the damage. Motorists are advised to take alternate routes and expect delays.
Content:
The Saskatoon Police Service advises that City of Saskatoon engineers have assessed the damage, and one eastbound lane on Circle Drive and one northbound lane of Highway 11 have been opened for traffic to proceed.

The Saskatoon Police Service Collision Analyst Unit, in cooperation with the Saskatchewan Highway Patrol, is investigating the collision. No injuries were reported.

Traffic flow restrictions will remain until further notice. Motorists are asked to take alternate routes or expect delays in the area.

The police also reported that a semi with a trailer carrying equipment traveling northbound on Highway 11 from Regina into the city limits at the Circle Drive overpass was too high for the structure, causing significant damage.

As a result, the northbound lanes of Highway 11 into the city and the eastbound lanes of Circle Drive over the bridge will be closed until engineers from the City of Saskatoon assess the damage and determine if it is safe for travel. Motorists are advised to take alternate routes until further notice.

Title: Sask. Party government under fire for $10-a-day child care cutoff
Author: Michael Joel-Hansen
Summary: Saskatchewan's government announced that only children turning six after April 1 will qualify for $10-a-day childcare, leaving families with children born before that date facing higher fees. Critics, including NDP Leader Carla Beck, criticized the cutoff, which increases costs for some families by up to $680 monthly. Education Minister Hindley called the deal a negotiated improvement despite its limitations.
Content:
Saskatchewan NDP Leader Carla Beck criticized the provincial government after it announced that only children who turn six and are born after April 1 will qualify for the $10-a-day childcare rate, leaving some families facing full fees.

Tina Lloyd had been hopeful she’d have some monthly breathing room in her family’s budget when she heard that six-year-olds in their kindergarten year would be covered for $10-a-day child care under the upcoming agreement between the provincial and federal governments. The coverage was expected to extend until the end of kindergarten. Lloyd, whose daughter has a March 31 birthday, and her husband are now facing increased care fees, which will rise from $217 each month to up to $900 for full-time care or $650 for half-time care. The provincial government recently informed parents and providers that the agreement covers $10-a-day child care only for children who turn six after April 1, when the new deal takes effect.

Under the new provisions, children with birthdays before April 1 will not be covered, and families will face an additional $680 in costs starting less than a month from now. When the $1.6 billion funding agreement was announced in November 2025, Education Minister Everett Hindley emphasized that ensuring children did not age out of the program at six was a major priority. He stated that if the deal were to be renegotiated, this was an aspect they would try to include.

Speaking with media in Regina on Thursday, Hindley acknowledged that the agreement was “not perfect” but considered it an improvement over previous arrangements. He explained that the deal resulted from negotiations between the provincial and federal governments, which involved compromises. Hindley described it as “the best deal that we were able to obtain” and did not see the cutoff as an oversight by the province.

Cara Werner, chair of the advocacy group Childcare Now Saskatchewan and director of Dream Big Rocanville, told reporters that providers, parents, and caregivers had believed that six-year-olds born before April 1 would be covered through kindergarten. She criticized the timing of the information about the cutoff, suggesting it reflected poorly on Hindley’s understanding of the deal.

Saskatchewan NDP Leader Carla Beck stated that caregivers whose children are excluded from the program will face costs between $30 and $85 more per day than those whose children are included.

Title: Say Cheese! Meet SmashingConf Amsterdam 🇳🇱
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Iris Lješnjanin)
Summary: Smashing Conf Amsterdam 2026, a leading UX and front-end conference, will be held from April 13-16 at Pathé Tuschinski. Since 2012, it emphasizes practical, live demonstrations by industry experts on topics like design systems, accessibility, and AI. In-person tickets cost €724.79, with discounts available, while online access is €200.
Content:
Smashing Conf Amsterdam 2026 is a premier conference for designers and UI engineers focusing on UX and front-end topics such as design systems, accessibility, Figma, UX writing, modern CSS, and AI. The event will take place from April 13 to 16, 2026, at the Pathé Tuschinski in Amsterdam, the Netherlands.

Since its inception in 2012, Smashing Conf has emphasized practical, real-world workflows. Unlike traditional conferences that rely on polished slide decks, Smashing Conf encourages speakers to demonstrate their actual work processes live—coding, designing, and troubleshooting in real-time—to provide actionable insights.

The conference aims to foster a spirit of curiosity and community, celebrating the Dutch concept of "Gezelligheid," which encompasses coziness, conviviality, and a warm, inviting atmosphere. Attendees can expect to learn from industry experts, see live demonstrations, and engage with the latest trends in design and development.

Tickets are available in-person for €724.79, with options to save up to 25% through a Smashing Membership. An online pass costing €200 provides access to live talks and behind-the-scenes content, including video recordings.

Title: The SIP Formula Is Broken and Everyone Knows It
Author: Phil Mackintosh
Summary: The article explains that the SIP (Securities Information Processor) formula is flawed, as it rewards data providers without adding corresponding value for investors. While the SIP has improved transparency and liquidity, its current economics favor professional and electronic traders over retail investors. Research indicates that quotes are twice as important as trades in price discovery, highlighting the misalignment in the current system.
Content:
Market Infrastructure: The SIP Formula Is Broken and Everyone Knows It

February 26, 2026 — 05:20 pm EST

Investors around the world know that it's important to know what the right prices for stocks are, and ideally, that they don’t end up paying too much for the stocks they are buying. In the U.S., solving that problem included creating a consolidated tape (also known as Securities Information Processor, or SIP) and a National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) as part of Reg NMS.

The SIP has been undeniably good for the public. By consolidating all the best prices, it protected investors, reduced trading costs and complexity. That, in turn, arguably improved liquidity, helping attract more companies to U.S. public markets. But the NBBO isn’t a “free service.” It is a result of venues and traders who decide to advertise their liquidity publicly. To their credit, regulators at the time realized that those providing data to the public should be rewarded and incentivized.

However, as we show today, the market has evolved in ways that take advantage of economics—usually without adding the desired compensating value to the public, who expect that they are paying for good NBBO quotes. The SIP formula works to reward those providing its data. We have detailed how the SIP economics work here. At a basic level, the SIP charges different kinds of users different prices based on how much that data helps those users. Professionals pay more than retail investors. Electronic trading systems pay more than professionals. It then rewards those who contribute data to the SIP. Because both quotes and trades contain essential signals about a stock’s value and market interest, the current formula splits revenue evenly: 50% to trades, 50% to quotes, with relatively more money allocated to less-liquid stocks that need market makers more. The totals are shown in Chart 1.

Chart 1: The SIP charges data users and pays data producers based on a formula. Note that “non-display” data, which we will talk about later, is based on the “use” (for routing, trade pricing, and benchmarking) of the data by computers, rather than display (on screens) for brokers or customers. This suggests quotes are twice as important as trades.

A number of well-known academics have thought deeply about price discovery—and many are also pretty good at math. What their research shows is that, for most stocks, quotes matter around twice as much as trades. This makes sense when you consider that the trades in the market are often using NBBO prices. (For example, midpoint trades only occur at that price because it’s in the middle of the NBBO at the time.) Chart 2: Some research suggests quotes are twice as important as trades. The more detailed math done in Brogaard’s study is of interest.

Title: Now Shipping: Accessible UX Research, A New Smashing Book By Michele Williams
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Ari Stiles)
Summary: *Accessible UX Research* by Dr. Michele Williams offers a practical guide to inclusive user research, emphasizing testing with diverse users and assistive technologies. It provides tips for beginners on asking better questions and managing biases, and detailed insights for accessibility professionals and developers. The book aims to help create more effective, inclusive digital products through comprehensive research strategies.
Content:
Good UX research is at the root of great products. It takes the guesswork out of our designs and helps us solve problems before they grow. One of the best ways to make our research effective is to keep it inclusive—testing with users with different needs and abilities, and using their feedback to build products that work for more people.

Our newest book, *Accessible UX Research*, can help you plan and execute great user research. Dr. Michele Williams draws from years of experience to build a clear, easy-to-follow roadmap. This book has something for everyone who wants to build digital products well:

If you are just getting started with research, you will find helpful tips for making your research more inclusive. You will also get a primer on how to ask better questions, understand your own biases, and how to use your limited time and budget effectively.

If you are an accessibility-minded professional, you will find a deep well of details on different assistive technologies, how to include them in your testing environment, and ways to record and share your results to create a real impact on the products you make.

If you are a developer, a manager, or someone who wants to understand how different abilities impact each user’s experience, you will find the history, clear descriptions, and cultural touchpoints you need to make sense of all the accessibility and inclusion recommendations you encounter.

The book isn’t a checklist for you to complete as part of your accessibility work. It’s a practical guide to inclusive UX research, from start to finish. If you’ve ever felt unsure how to include disabled participants, or worried about “getting it wrong,” this book is for you. You’ll get clear, practical strategies to make your research more inclusive, effective, and reliable.

Inside, you’ll learn how to plan research.

Title: Filament v5.3.0 Released with Deferred Tab Badges and Column Manager Improvements
Author: Paul Redmond
Summary: Filament v5.3.0 introduces ten new features, including deferred tab badge loading for improved performance, flexible column manager modals, customizable Rich Editor plugins, and visual sorting indicators on tables. It also adds expanded rate limiting options, support for PHP enums in navigation, a new controls slot in Callout components, and extended color palettes. Bug fixes address sidebar state restoration and missing translations.
Content:
Filament v5.3.0 introduces ten new features, including deferred tab badge loading, column manager modal and slideover support, enhancements to Rich Editor plugins, and sort loading indicators on table columns.

Deferred tab badge loading allows badge counts to load independently from tab content, improving performance by avoiding unnecessary database queries on page load when badge values are expensive to compute.

The column manager now supports displaying in a modal or slideover, providing layout options beyond the default dropdown for managing table column visibility.

Rich Editor plugins can now supply their own file attachment providers, enabling custom upload and storage behavior directly within the editor without external configuration. Additionally, plugins can control the visibility and state of toolbar buttons for more granular editing controls.

Table columns now feature a loading spinner on sort icons during sorting operations, offering visual feedback when sorting takes a moment to complete.

Rate limiting configurations have been expanded to support user ID and email address keys, complementing existing IP-based options. Navigation groups now accept Unit Enum values, simplifying the definition of navigation groups using PHP enums.

The Callout component has gained a controls slot, allowing custom action buttons or content to be added alongside callout messages. Resources now support additional configuration options, and the component color palette has been extended with more Tailwind CSS color options.

Bug fixes include correct restoration of sidebar state on desktop page loads and the addition of missing chart widget translations.

Title: Evade Police/Impaired Driving - McPherson Ave, Preston Ave.
Author: Unknown
Summary: A 41-year-old Saskatoon man faces multiple charges after evading police during a reckless incident on March 4, 2026. After a traffic stop, he fled on foot and in a vehicle, which was driven dangerously and damaged tires. He was arrested, found to be wanted on warrants, and suspected of being under the influence of methamphetamine. Charges include impaired driving, dangerous driving, evading police, and breaching court conditions.
Content:
A 41-year-old Saskatoon man is facing multiple charges following an incident in which a driver evaded police overnight.

At approximately 1:00 a.m., March 4, 2026, an officer on patrol spotted a suspicious vehicle in the 500 block of 8th Street East. The officer attempted a traffic stop of a black Chrysler 200 when it turned onto McPherson Avenue. The driver was slow to pull over, and when he did, a male and a female exited the vehicle and fled on foot. The driver then sped away as the officer approached the vehicle. The suspect vehicle evaded police and was driven recklessly in the area of Wilson Crescent and Preston Avenue. 

The driver attempted to overtake a snowplow but lost control after tires had been damaged earlier in the incident, ending up on the median. The male then tried to flee on foot but was apprehended. He was found to be wanted on several warrants and is believed to have been under the influence of methamphetamine at the time.

The man is now charged with possession of bear spray, impaired driving, dangerous driving, evading police, and breaching court conditions twice.

Title: Update - Additional Arrest - Homicide - 1700 Block 20th Street West
Author: Unknown
Summary: A 26-year-old man has been charged with aggravated assault and manslaughter in connection with a February 3, 2026, homicide and will appear in court. An 18-year-old previously charged with manslaughter was arrested on February 26, 2026, and now faces charges including accessory after the fact, public mischief, and obstruction of justice, with manslaughter and assault charges withdrawn. The investigation continues.
Content:
A second male has been charged in connection to a February 3, 2026, homicide. A 26-year-old male is charged with aggravated assault and manslaughter. He will appear in court this afternoon.

A man previously charged in the homicide investigation is now facing new charges. On February 26, 2026, investigators attended a correctional facility in Saskatoon and arrested an 18-year-old man who was previously charged with manslaughter in relation to the February 3rd homicide. He is now charged with accessory after the fact to manslaughter, accessory after the fact to aggravated assault, public mischief, and obstruction of justice. The charges of manslaughter and aggravated assault have been withdrawn.

The Major Crime Unit continues to investigate.

Title: Grading the Maple Leafs & Kings’ Scott Laughton Trade
Author: Spencer Lazary
Summary: The Toronto Maple Leafs traded Scott Laughton to the Los Angeles Kings for a conditional 2026 third-round pick, aiming to recoup draft capital and free cap space. Over 24 hours, they also traded Nicolas Roy and Bobby McMann for draft picks. The moves reflect a strategy to sell assets and rebuild through the draft, with the Kings adding depth for a playoff push.
Content:
The Toronto Maple Leafs left everything to the final buzzer. All day, Leafs Nation was wondering what deals the organization would make to help this team retool. Yesterday, they traded Nicolas Roy to the Colorado Avalanche, and today they dealt Bobby McMann to the Seattle Kraken. Now, they have announced that they have traded Scott Laughton to the Los Angeles Kings.

Laughton was acquired just one year ago at the 2025 NHL Trade Deadline. Now, he is on his way out of town and heading to California to try and help the Kings push for a playoff spot down the stretch. As for the Maple Leafs, their selling of assets continues.

Full Trade:
Maple Leafs receive: Conditional 2026 third-round pick
Kings receive: Scott Laughton

Maple Leafs Restocking Draft Capital
Trade Grade: C

The Maple Leafs have made three trades over the last 24 hours, and all of them have the return be draft picks. That is ideal for Toronto, who are trying to maximize cap space heading into next season but also aim to recoup draft capital for players on expiring contracts. No fan within Leafs Nation should have expected them to pull off a hockey trade. It was always going to be a cap dump for draft picks or a massive trade for someone like Robert Thomas. It makes more sense for them to sell assets for picks and then look at how they can retool this roster in the offseason rather than trying to fix it at the deadline.

Brad Treliving did have the right approach in selling their UFAs. Unfortunately, he had to budge on his asking price. He was able to get a first and a fifth for Roy, and a second and a fourth for McMann. However, he was only able to get a conditional third-round pick, which slides to a second if the Kings make the playoffs. While that is a decent return, it is significantly less than what he paid for Laughton just a season ago, which was Nikita Grebennik and a conditional 2027 first-round pick. It would have been a better return if he could have gotten a player like Alex Turcotte or even a mid-tier prospect as well as the conditional pick. Regardless, he was able to sell an expiring asset, which should set them up well in the draft. The only hope now is that the Kings can play well enough to get into the playoffs.

From the Kings’ perspective, the cost for Laughton is minimal. They are giving up a conditional third-round pick, which could become a second if they make the playoffs, and are acquiring a reliable depth center to bolster their lineup as they push for a postseason spot.

Title: Grading the Islanders’ Trade for the Blues’ Brayden Schenn
Author: Alex Chauvancy
Summary: The New York Islanders traded for Brayden Schenn from the St. Louis Blues, giving up a 2026 first-round pick, Jonathan Drouin, and a goaltending prospect. The move, involving Schenn's full no-trade clause waiver, is seen as questionable due to Schenn's declining performance, contract, and impact on team speed. The Blues received a strong return, maximizing Schenn's value.
Content:
The New York Islanders have had an interesting few weeks ahead of the NHL trade deadline. Shortly before the Olympic break, they acquired Ondřej Palát and a couple of draft picks from the New Jersey Devils in exchange for Maxim Tsyplakov. Today, they made one of the splashier deals of Deadline Day, acquiring Brayden Schenn from the St. Louis Blues.

Here are the full details:

Islanders acquire: Schenn  
Blues acquire: 2026 1st-round pick, 3rd-round pick, Jonathan Drouin, goaltending prospect Marcus Gildon

Schenn had to waive his no-trade clause to join the Islanders, but it may have been better for the Islanders had he invoked it. This move is considered a head-scratcher that doesn’t make much sense for where they are as an organization. As for the Blues, they made out well and got full value for their longtime captain.

The Islanders have been a surprise this season and look like a safe bet to make the playoffs. That’s why GM Mathieu Darche decided to become a buyer, but it’s questionable whether his moves have made the team significantly better. Palát’s best days seem behind him, and the same is true for Schenn.

Schenn has 12 goals and 28 points in 61 games this season, which is decent production, but there are red flags. His skating has declined in recent seasons, which is concerning given the Islanders’ uptempo style. He also struggles in transition. His shooting ability has also fallen off over the last couple of seasons, though he remains a good playmaker and excels in faceoffs, which may have influenced the Islanders’ decision to acquire him.

Still, there are more cons than pros in this deal. One issue is Schenn’s contract, which runs through next season at a cap hit of $6.5 million. This trade would have been more palatable if he were a rental, but the extra year complicates matters. Additionally, he makes the Islanders a slower team, adding to concerns about their pace, especially since Palát already did that.

The Islanders also gave up quite a bit to acquire Schenn. Drouin struggled with the Islanders and is unlikely to be missed much, but giving up a first-round pick for a declining player in his mid-30s with term is questionable asset management. Overall, skepticism remains about whether this trade will work out as intended.

The Blues, on the other hand, maximized Schenn’s value. They avoided issues with no-trade clauses, as Colton Parayko’s trade was blocked, and received a solid return for their longtime captain. Drouin has been productive at times over the last few seasons, despite struggling with the Islanders.

Title: Avalanche Acquire Nazem Kadri From Calgary Flames
Author: Matteo Giuliano
Summary: Nazem Kadri was traded from the Calgary Flames to the Colorado Avalanche for a 2028 first-round pick, a 2027 second-round pick, Victor Olofsson, and Max Curran. The Flames will retain 20% of Kadri’s $7 million salary. The Avalanche bolster their center depth, making them strong Stanley Cup contenders, while the Flames acquire valuable future assets.
Content:
The trade deadline saw a significant move with Nazem Kadri and a 2027 fourth-round pick being traded to the Colorado Avalanche. The Calgary Flames received a 2028 first-round pick, a 2027 second-round pick, Victor Olofsson, and Max Curran. The Flames will retain 20 percent of Kadri’s contract, which is $1.4 million, as part of the deal.

What the Avalanche Are Getting
Avalanche fans are already familiar with Kadri, who is considered an ideal third-line centre. He provides a physical presence and has demonstrated the ability to score goals even on a low-scoring team. This season, his stats include 10 goals and 29 assists in 59 games, though he scored 35 goals last season for the Flames.

Trade talks around Kadri have been ongoing since his first year in Calgary, which coincided with a difficult period for the team. The Jonathan Huberdeau trade in the same offseason is now regarded as one of the worst trades in NHL history. Kadri is a talented player, but concerns about his age and contract remain. He is 35 and under contract for three more seasons at an average annual value of $7 million. Despite the high salary cap, winning a Stanley Cup with Kadri could justify the expense.

A New Stanley Cup Contender?
The Avalanche’s biggest weakness before the deadline was the centre position. With the trade, they acquired additional depth at third- and fourth-line centre. The lineup now includes Nathan MacKinnon, Brock Nelson, Kadri, and Nicolas Roy, creating a strong core of centres. With this improved depth, Colorado is now considered a top contender to win their second Stanley Cup in five years, especially given the league’s wide-open competition this season.

The Flames Continue Their Rebuild
The Flames gained a first- and second-round pick, adding to their collection of six first-round selections over the next three seasons. These picks could serve as assets for future trades, as it’s unlikely all will be used in the first round. They also received prospect Max Curran, who has posted 14 goals and 27 assists for 41 points in 31 games with the Edmonton Oil Kings.

Flames general manager Craig Conroy managed to acquire valuable assets despite concerns over Kadri’s contract length, price, and age.

Title: Ducks Trade Ryan Strome to Flames for 7th-Round Pick
Author: Owen Hillman
Summary: The Anaheim Ducks traded forward Ryan Strome to the Calgary Flames for a 2027 seventh-round pick, gaining cap relief. Strome, signed for $25 million in 2022, saw declining production and was sidelined by injuries. The Flames, rebuilding, seek veteran leadership and positional flexibility, while the Ducks focus on developing prospects and managing cap space.
Content:
The Anaheim Ducks have traded forward Ryan Strome to the Calgary Flames for a 2027 seventh-round pick, according to Pierre Le Brun. The Ducks get some cap relief and move off of a bad contract, while the Flames acquire a veteran to mentor potential young players in their core.

The Ducks signed Strome to a five-year, $25 million contract on July 13, 2022. This deal, along with contracts for Frank Vatrano, Alex Killorn, and Radko Gudas, was intended to set the tone for the team's culture. With promising forward prospects like Mason McTavish, Leo Carlsson, and Cutter Gauthier, Strome was expected to serve as a mentor.

He was still a decent contributor with Anaheim, averaging 41 points between the 2022-23 and 2024-25 seasons. However, his production declined significantly this season, with just three goals and three assists through 33 games. Part of that decline was due to injury; he sustained an upper-body injury in the preseason that sidelined him for the first 16 games, and he has been dealing with illnesses in March.

Despite his struggles, Strome remains under contract at $5 million per season until 2027. With Carlsson and McTavish developing into franchise players and the team signing Mikael Granlund to a three-year, $21 million deal in 2025, Strome’s role with the Ducks was diminishing.

The Ducks were only able to receive a seventh-round pick for Strome, but they gained some cap relief. They have over $49 million in cap space this season but need to finalize deals with pending restricted free agents Carlsson, Gauthier, Oren Zellweger, and Pavel Mintyukov.

The Flames, meanwhile, are in rebuild mode and sought veteran leadership through this trade. They did not sell off completely at the deadline, with Blake Coleman remaining on the roster. With 55 points, they are positioned to secure a top draft pick in the 2026 NHL Draft, tied for the second-highest lottery odds with the St. Louis Blues, behind the Vancouver Canucks.

In addition to acquiring Strome, Calgary traded Nazem Kadri, Rasmus Andersson, and MacKenzie Weegar for strong returns. Strome could also rebound with the Flames, taking on an elevated role and providing positional flexibility, having played both wing and center. He still possesses playmaking abilities that could benefit young players like Matthew Coronato and Connor Zary.

Calgary didn’t give up much to acquire him and retains significant draft capital, including two first-round picks and four second-round picks.

Title: Sources: Ravens to acquire Crosby in blockbuster
Author: Jamison Hensley
Summary: The Baltimore Ravens are trading first-round picks in 2026 and 2027 to acquire five-time Pro Bowl defensive end Maxx Crosby from the Las Vegas Raiders, contingent on passing a physical. This marks Baltimore’s first trade of a veteran for a first-round pick. Crosby, 28, will bolster the Ravens' pass rush after a poor 2025 season, aiming to improve their postseason prospects.
Content:
In the biggest trade in franchise history, the Baltimore Ravens are acquiring five-time Pro Bowl defensive end Maxx Crosby from the Las Vegas Raiders for first-round picks in 2026 and 2027, sources told ESPN's Adam Schefter on Friday night. The deal is contingent on Crosby passing a physical, which can't occur until the start of the new league year Wednesday.

This marks the first time in the Ravens' 31-year existence that they've used a first-round pick to trade for a veteran player. Baltimore had the No. 14 pick in this year's draft. Among the teams the Ravens beat out for Crosby were the Dallas Cowboys, who were willing to offer a first- and a second-round pick for the star pass rusher.

Crosby, 28, significantly upgrades a struggling Ravens pass rush that in 2025 totaled 30 sacks, Baltimore's fewest in 15 years. Last season was also the first time since the Ravens' inaugural 1996 campaign that they didn't have an edge rusher with more than 4.5 sacks.

The Ravens are making this uncharacteristic offseason splash after a disappointing 2025 season in which they finished 8-9, failed to make the playoffs, and then fired longtime coach John Harbaugh. Baltimore has been looking for a game changer, such as Crosby, to get over the postseason hurdle and reach the Super Bowl for the first time since the 2012 season.

The addition of Crosby gives the Ravens their most decorated and feared pass rusher in his prime since Terrell Suggs, the franchise's all-time sacks leader, who played in Baltimore from 2003 to 2018. Crosby has produced four double-digit sack seasons in his seven-year career. Over that span, the Ravens have had two edge rushers with 10 or more sacks in a season: Kyle Van Noy (12.5 in 2024) and Odafe Oweh (10 in 2024).
